stem: What is the purpose of spoiling in a gradient-echo sequence?

options.text: To eliminate longitudinal magnetization permanently
To destroy residual transverse coherence before the next excitation
To refocus susceptibility dephasing into a true spin echo
To increase the fat-water frequency difference

explanation: RF or gradient spoiling prevents leftover transverse magnetization from contributing coherently to later signals. The resulting contrast is dominated more predictably by T1 and the fresh excitation.

stem: What distinguishes a coherent gradient-echo sequence?

options.text: All transverse magnetization is deliberately randomized after every readout
Only longitudinal recovery contributes to signal
Residual transverse magnetization is preserved and refocused across repetitions
A 180-degree pulse removes every off-resonance effect

explanation: Coherent sequences retain and reuse transverse components, allowing multiple echo pathways and steady-state behavior. This differs from spoiled sequences that intentionally erase that history.

stem: Which feature is characteristic of balanced steady-state free precession?

options.text: No gradients are applied
Residual transverse magnetization is completely spoiled
A long TR allows full recovery before every pulse
All gradient moments are balanced within each repetition

explanation: Balanced gradients return stationary spins to the same phase position after each TR, sustaining a high-signal steady state. The method is rapid and produces contrast related to the T2/T1 ratio.

stem: Which tissue or structure is often conspicuously bright on balanced SSFP images?

options.text: Fluid or blood with a high T2-to-T1 ratio
Cortical bone with almost no mobile protons
Air with no detectable hydrogen signal
Metal because susceptibility is fully corrected

explanation: Balanced SSFP provides high signal efficiency and favors tissues with a high T2/T1 ratio, including many fluids and blood. Bone and air remain low signal.

stem: What artifact commonly results from off-resonance in balanced SSFP?

options.text: Pure phase wrap independent of FOV
Dark banding
Complete fat suppression at every field strength
A uniform doubling of slice thickness

explanation: Balanced SSFP signal varies periodically with off-resonance frequency, creating dark bands where local field offsets fall at signal nulls. Good shimming and shorter TR reduce this problem.

stem: How is magnetization stored during formation of a stimulated echo?

options.text: A gradient permanently removes the longitudinal component
The receiver converts T2 decay directly into B0
An RF pulse places part of the transverse coherence along the longitudinal axis
A single 180-degree pulse eliminates all prior phase information

explanation: A sequence of RF pulses can convert transverse phase information into longitudinal magnetization and later return it to the transverse plane. This stored pathway produces a stimulated echo with different relaxation behavior.
